title: Nothing feels fun anymore
text: People may say nothing really feels fun anymore, even things they used to look forward to. Hobbies, outings, or small pleasures can start to feel flat or empty.

title: Losing interest in usual activities
text: A common sign is losing interest in activities that once mattered. Someone may stop engaging with hobbies, routines, or social plans because they no longer feel rewarding.

title: Still doing things without enjoyment
text: A person may keep doing normal activities but describe them as mechanical or joyless. The activity continues, but the sense of pleasure or involvement is missing.

title: Not really into anything lately
text: Someone might say they are not really into anything lately or that they do not care much about what they used to enjoy. This can reflect a mild or early loss of interest.

title: Things do not feel the same
text: People sometimes describe a vague change where things just do not feel the same anymore. Activities may seem dull, less meaningful, or harder to get emotionally connected to.

title: Feeling down most of the time
text: A person may describe feeling down, low, or sad much of the day. The mood tends to linger and is not easily lifted by normal positive events.

title: Feeling off for a while
text: Some people do not directly say they feel sad but instead say they have felt off, not like themselves, or emotionally different lately. This can reflect a depressed mood in less direct language.

title: Heavy or weighed down feeling
text: Depressed mood can sound like feeling heavy, drained, or emotionally weighed down. The person may struggle to explain it clearly but describe a constant low feeling.

title: Sadness that sticks around
text: Rather than a brief reaction to one event, the sadness tends to stay around. It may return day after day and interfere with normal routines, motivation, or social interaction.

title: Crying more or feeling close to tears
text: Some individuals report crying more easily or feeling like they could cry without a clear reason. This can reflect ongoing emotional distress and low mood.

title: Trouble falling asleep
text: Sleep problems may show up as lying awake for a long time before sleep. The person may feel tired but still unable to settle or shut their mind down.

title: Waking up during the night
text: A person may wake up repeatedly during the night or too early in the morning and have trouble getting back to sleep. Broken sleep often leaves them feeling unrefreshed.

title: Sleeping much more than usual
text: Some people sleep more than usual or spend a lot of time in bed but still feel tired. Extra sleep does not necessarily bring back energy.

title: Sleep schedule feels off
text: A person may say their sleep has been off lately, with noticeable changes in timing, duration, or quality. This kind of shift can be part of a depressive pattern.

title: Tired all the time
text: People may say they are tired all the time, even when they have not done much. The exhaustion can feel constant and make ordinary tasks seem harder than they should.

title: Everything takes too much effort
text: A common description is that everything feels like too much effort. Small responsibilities can start to feel unusually draining or difficult to get through.

title: No energy for basic tasks
text: Low energy may affect basic daily activities such as getting dressed, cleaning, working, or answering messages. The problem is not just being busy but feeling depleted.

title: Feeling worn out without clear cause
text: Some people describe being worn out or run down without a clear physical reason. The tiredness can be both physical and mental, with little sense of recovery.

title: Not feeling like eating
text: A person may say they just do not feel like eating or that food does not sound good. Meals can feel unimportant, effortful, or easy to skip.

title: Eating more for comfort
text: Some individuals eat more than usual, especially when feeling emotionally low. Increased eating may be tied to comfort, distraction, or reduced control around food.

title: Noticeable change in eating habits
text: Depression can involve a clear shift in appetite or eating routine, either up or down. The change is usually noticeable compared with the person’s usual pattern.

title: Feeling like a burden
text: A person may say they feel like a burden to other people or that others would be better off without their problems. This kind of self-evaluation often reflects worthlessness.

title: Feeling not good enough
text: People may describe themselves as not good enough, useless, or disappointing. These beliefs can be persistent and much harsher than the situation actually supports.

title: Blaming self for too much
text: Excessive guilt can show up as taking too much responsibility for mistakes, conflicts, or negative outcomes. The person may blame themselves even when it is not fair or realistic.

title: Feeling ashamed of oneself
text: Some individuals speak about feeling ashamed of who they are, not just what they did. This deeper negative self-judgment can be a strong depressive signal.

title: Hard to focus on anything
text: A person may say it is hard to focus on anything, even simple conversations or tasks. Their attention may drift easily, and thinking can feel less steady than usual.

title: Mind feels foggy
text: People sometimes describe their mind as foggy, cloudy, or slow. Thoughts may feel harder to organize, and it can take longer to understand or respond.

title: Trouble making simple decisions
text: Depression can make even small decisions feel difficult or tiring. The person may hesitate, overthink, or avoid choosing because their thinking feels slowed down.

title: Losing track easily
text: Someone may lose track of what they were saying, reading, or trying to do. This kind of mental slipping can reflect concentration problems rather than lack of effort.

title: Moving or speaking more slowly
text: Some people appear slowed down in how they move, speak, or respond. Their pace may feel noticeably reduced, as if everything takes longer than usual.

title: Restless and unable to settle
text: Others show the opposite pattern and seem restless, agitated, or unable to relax. They may fidget, pace, or feel uncomfortable sitting still.

title: Taking longer to respond
text: A person may pause more, answer more slowly, or seem delayed in conversation. This can reflect slowed mental and physical processing in depression.

title: Passive thoughts about death
text: Some people have thoughts that life is not worth much or that it would be easier not to wake up. These thoughts can be passive and still signal serious distress.

title: Not wanting to be here
text: A person may say they do not want to be here anymore or that they wish they could disappear. This indirect wording can reflect suicidal thinking and should be taken seriously.

title: Thoughts of self-harm or suicide
text: More direct expressions can include thoughts of hurting oneself, ending one’s life, or thinking about suicide. These statements indicate high-risk depressive distress.

title: Not wanting to talk to people
text: People may say they do not really want to talk to people lately or that conversations feel like too much. This can reflect withdrawal rather than ordinary shyness.

title: Pulling back from others
text: A person may gradually pull back from friends, family, or social activities they normally keep up with. The pattern is often a reduction in contact, not just one missed event.

title: Spending more time alone than usual
text: Depression may show up as spending more time alone and avoiding interaction, even with familiar people. Isolation becomes more common than the person’s usual pattern.

title: Hard to get started on anything
text: A person may know what they need to do but still feel unable to get started. This low drive is often described as having no push, no spark, or no reason to begin.

title: No real drive lately
text: People may say they have had no real drive lately, even for tasks that matter. This loss of initiative can affect work, school, chores, and self-care.

title: Feeling like nothing will get better
text: Hopelessness often sounds like believing nothing will get better, no matter what changes. The person may see the future as blank, bleak, or permanently stuck.

title: Hard to imagine a better future
text: A person may struggle to picture things improving or may feel there is nothing ahead to look forward to. This goes beyond temporary discouragement.

title: Giving up on things changing
text: Someone may sound as if they have given up on things changing. This can appear in statements that effort feels pointless or that improvement does not seem possible.

title: Feeling numb instead of sad
text: Not everyone describes depression as sadness. Some people say they feel numb, blank, or emotionally shut down, with less reaction to things that would normally matter.

title: Going through the motions
text: A person may describe just going through the motions without feeling much. They continue daily life, but it feels automatic, detached, or emotionally empty.

title: Feeling like there is no point
text: A person may wonder what the point of things is or say that nothing feels meaningful anymore. This kind of statement can reflect depressive loss of purpose.

title: No sense of direction
text: Some individuals describe feeling lost, aimless, or without direction in a way that feels emotionally heavy. The concern is not just uncertainty but a deeper loss of meaning.

title: Turning problems back on oneself
text: A person may quickly assume things are their fault, even when the situation is shared or unclear. This style of self-blame can reinforce depressed thinking.

title: Harsh self-criticism
text: Some people talk to themselves in very critical ways, focusing on mistakes, flaws, or perceived failures. The tone is often more severe than the facts justify.

title: Seeing oneself in a deeply negative way
text: Depression can involve a stable negative view of oneself, such as feeling broken, inadequate, or unlikeable. These beliefs often feel true to the person even when others disagree.

title: Feeling like something is wrong with me
text: A person may say they feel like something is wrong with them in a broad personal sense. This reflects negative self-perception rather than one specific complaint.

title: Small problems feel huge
text: A person may react to setbacks as if they mean everything is falling apart. Minor problems can quickly feel overwhelming, final, or impossible to recover from.

title: Expecting the worst outcome
text: Depression can involve expecting the worst even from ordinary situations. The mind moves quickly toward negative conclusions and threatening possibilities.

title: One setback becomes a bigger story
text: A single bad event may be taken as proof that nothing ever works out or that the future will go the same way. This broad negative leap is a common depressive thinking pattern.

title: Negative thoughts that keep looping
text: Some people describe negative thoughts that keep looping in their mind and are hard to shut off. The repetition can intensify low mood and make it harder to focus on anything else.

title: Mind keeps going back to the bad stuff
text: A person may keep returning to regrets, worries, or painful thoughts even when they want to move on. This repeated mental pull toward negative material is clinically relevant.

title: Getting irritated more easily
text: Depression does not always sound sad. Some people notice they are getting irritated, impatient, or short with others more easily than usual.

title: Small things set me off lately
text: A person may say small things set them off lately or that they feel easily bothered. This can reflect depressive distress, especially when it marks a change from their usual behavior.

title: Feeling stuck and unable to move forward
text: People may describe feeling stuck, trapped, or unable to move forward in life. The feeling often includes helplessness, low energy, and difficulty imagining change.
